## pqac-00000039

From text 'suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 63-66'.

FK Suleiman. Improving pyrometry of advanced high strength steels during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2025.

During intercritical annealing of advanced high-strength steels (AHSS), selective oxide formation (composition, morphology, thickness) substantially alters spectral emissivity. Oxide layers are typically thin (40–320 nm) and often dielectric/non-absorbing; when the film thickness is smaller than or comparable to the pyrometry wavelength (d ≤ λ) thin-film interference occurs: reflections from the film interfaces interfere constructively or destructively, producing wavelength-dependent oscillations in emissivity. The effect is stronger for uniform films with lower refractive index and weaker when the surface is discontinuous or nodular. For wavelengths much larger than the oxide thickness (λ >> d), surface roughness and oxide nodules dominate, increasing emissivity via multiple scattering. Emissivity also depends on temperature (Drude/Hagen-Rubens behavior for metals) and on process variables (alloy composition, annealing atmosphere/dew point) that control oxide growth; experiments show oxide growth can increase emissivity and that thinner oxides can give lower emissivity. These mechanisms explain observed spectral emissivity oscillations during oxide-scale growth and complicate pyrometric temperature measurement unless the oxide state is accounted for.

The excerpt explains how oxide scale growth during annealing strongly modifies spectral emissivity of advanced high-strength steels (AHSS). Oxide layers are thin (40–320 nm) and can form as uniform film-like layers or grainy/nodular morphologies; both may coexist and alter EM interactions differently. When film thickness is on the order of or smaller than the pyrometry wavelength (d ≤ λ), thin-film interference occurs: reflections from the film interfaces are phase-shifted producing constructive and destructive interference that modulates (oscillates) spectral emissivity with wavelength and film thickness. A lower refractive index of a uniform oxide film increases interference effects and raises spectral emissivity. At wavelengths much larger than the oxide thickness (λ >> d), surface roughness and oxide nodules dominate, causing random scattering, multiple reflections and generally increased emissivity. Pure-metal temperature trends (Drude/Hagen-Rubens) are complicated by oxide composition, morphology, thickness, atmosphere (dew point) and alloying (selective Mn/Al oxidation), so emissivity can vary during annealing cycles; thinner oxides have been associated with lower emissivity. These mechanisms explain oscillatory spectral emissivity behavior as oxide scales grow and change morphology during annealing.

The excerpt reports that during intercritical annealing of DP980 steel, the evolving oxide scale strongly affects spectral emissivity. When oxide-film thickness is comparable to the detection wavelength, thin-film interference produces wavelength-dependent features in the measured emissivity (most pronounced at shorter wavelengths). The presence of metallic oxides (continuous films or nodules) alters surface state and emissivity; selective oxidation (segregation of Si, Mn, Cr, etc.) is thermodynamically favored under the tested dewpoints, and the resulting oxide properties depend on oxidation thermodynamics and kinetics. With increasing temperature above ~400°C the spectral emissivity decreases at all dewpoints (potentially due to the “crossover wavelength” or X-point phenomenon and temperature-dependent oxide behavior). At high peak annealing temperature (800°C) some samples (lowest dewpoint) show emissivity returning toward pre-annealed values for wavelengths >2 µm, suggesting oxide reduction or thinning. function.arguments: {"question": "What are the quantitative uncertainties reported for pyrometry temperature measurements during steel annealing and induction heating? What temperature errors result from emissivity uncertainty?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000047

From text 'suleiman2023effectofdewa pages 7-9'.

FK Suleiman, NS Narayanan, and KJ Daun. Effect of dew point on the evolving spectral emissivity of advanced high strength steel during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt reports a Bayesian (stochastic) treatment of emissivity uncertainty for pyrometric temperature estimation during intercritical annealing. The only explicit quantitative uncertainty reported is a credibility interval of ±21°C for the peak annealing temperature (PAT) when using the in situ-based stochastic prior; this interval is presented as compensating for emissivity variations with dewpoint and temperature. Emissivity priors are modeled as Gaussian distributions (99.7% within με ± 3σε), so values in low-probability tails lead to poor temperature estimates. The authors note systematic biases: all models underpredict soak temperature at −30°C and overpredict at the other dewpoints; whether an estimate under- or overpredicts depends on whether the prior mean is greater or less than the actual emissivity. Spectral emissivity trends reported (increased with dewpoint, decreased with temperature above 400°C, effects stronger below 2 μm and at −30°C) are cited as causes of pyrometry errors. No other specific numeric temperature-error magnitudes from emissivity uncertainty (besides ±21°C) are provided in the excerpt.


---

## pqac-00000048

From text 'suleiman2023effectofdew pages 7-9'.

FK Suleiman, NS Narayanan, and KJ Daun. Effect of dew point on the evolving spectral emissivity of advanced high strength steel during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt reports a quantified uncertainty for pyrometric peak annealing temperature (PAT) estimates: an in situ–based stochastic emissivity prior produced a credibility interval of ±21°C for the PAT. It also states that all models underpredict soak temperature at a −30°C dewpoint and overpredict at the other dewpoints, though no other explicit numeric temperature errors are given. Emissivity was modeled as a normal distribution (με ± σε), with 99.7% of observations expected within με ± 3σε; when actual emissivity lies in low-probability tails the Bayesian posterior assigns very low probability to those emissivity values, producing under- or overpredicted temperatures. The in situ prior underestimated emissivity variance at lower temperatures because its training range was 520–800°C; the ex situ prior (27 samples) also failed at −30°C, suggesting limited sample size or poor representation of in situ variation. Spectral emissivity increases with dewpoint (notably below 2 μm) and decreases with temperature above ~400°C, effects that drive the emissivity-related temperature errors. The excerpt does not discuss induction heating or provide additional quantitative temperature-error magnitudes beyond the ±21°C credibility interval.


---

## pqac-00000049

From text 'suleiman2023effectofdew pages 5-7'.

FK Suleiman, NS Narayanan, and KJ Daun. Effect of dew point on the evolving spectral emissivity of advanced high strength steel during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt describes how emissivity uncertainty is treated for multi-wavelength pyrometry during intercritical annealing of DP980 steel. Deterministic compensation algorithms give a single inferred temperature, while Bayesian (stochastic) models represent spectral emissivity as a probability density function and thus return a temperature pdf whose width reflects uncertainty. The study evaluated four models: two deterministic algorithms and two Gaussian priors (model #3 ex situ, model #4 in situ). Bayesian results report 95% credibility intervals (stated as corresponding to two standard deviations) and note that Gaussian priors imply 99.7% of observations within ±3σ. Temperature estimation used three wavelengths (1.6, 2.1, 2.4 μm). Qualitatively, steady-state estimates at 800°C increase with dewpoint (emissivity interpreted as higher temperature); the ex situ prior (model #3) underpredicts peak annealing temperatures, and most models overpredict during the heating ramp. The excerpt does not provide explicit numeric temperature uncertainties (°C) or concrete temperature-error magnitudes arising from emissivity uncertainty on these pages.


---

## pqac-00000050

From text 'suleiman2023effectofdewa pages 5-7'.

FK Suleiman, NS Narayanan, and KJ Daun. Effect of dew point on the evolving spectral emissivity of advanced high strength steel during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt describes how pyrometric temperature uncertainty during intercritical annealing of AHSS is treated: deterministic multi-wavelength (MW) compensation algorithms produce a single temperature (no uncertainty), whereas Bayesian models represent spectral emissivity as a probability distribution, producing a temperature pdf (MAP plus credibility intervals). Two stochastic emissivity priors are compared: an ex situ prior from 27 spectrometer measurements (model #3) and an in situ prior from 443 measurements (model #4). Temperatures were inferred using intensities at 1.6, 2.1 and 2.4 μm. The Bayesian results include 95% credibility intervals (stated as corresponding to two standard deviations) and the text notes the statistical property that ~99.7% of observations lie within ±3σ of the mean. Emissivity uncertainty leads to systematic temperature errors: e.g., steady-state estimates at 800°C are overpredicted with increasing dewpoint (oxide-enhanced emissivity misinterpreted as higher temperature), and several models overpredict during the heating ramp; model #3 showed the worst underprediction at −30°C. The excerpt does not report explicit numeric temperature-uncertainty magnitudes (°C) or specific temperature-error values resulting from emissivity uncertainty.


---

## pqac-00000051

From text 'giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 11-11'.

Nicola Giulietti, Gloria Cosoli, Rachele Napolitano, Giuseppe Pandarese, Gian Marco Revel, and Paolo Chiariotti. Spectral emissivity measurement for high-temperature applications: a systematic review. Acta IMEKO, 14:1-17, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846, doi:10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt provides measured uncertainty figures for pyrometry and radiation-thermometer based temperature measurements drawn from Table 5. Reported relative uncertainties and errors span the single-percent range: examples include a "Relative uncertainty in temperature < 2.5 %", specific pyrometers with "< 3 % (at 657 °C - IGAR12-LO)" and "< 3 % (at 462 °C - M76S)", and one device showing "≈ 0 % (M67S)". Two-colour and simulation-comparison entries cite "Approximately 1 % error between simulated and measured temperature" and an "Absolute temperature error < 2 % at 727 °C". General instrument performance notes state "Repeatability (1 %) and accuracy (< 2 %)" and a listed "Arithmetic error < 1 %" for another case. The table includes measurements on stainless steel and stainless steel 316L powder, indicating relevance to steel annealing/processing studies. The excerpt does not explicitly convert emissivity uncertainty into a direct temperature-error formula, but the listed pyrometry uncertainties (roughly 0–3%) represent overall measurement errors that will include emissivity-related contributions; specific temperature error due solely to an assumed emissivity change is not given in the provided text.

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a summary table of calibration/correction methods for pyrometry during induction heating and annealing of metals. Columns: Method, Principle, Advantages, Limitations, Key References. Rows should cover: (1) Blackbody reference calibration, (2) Thermocouple cross-calibration, (3) Ratio/dual-wavelength pyrometry, (4) Multi-wavelength pyrometry with polynomial emissivity models, (5) Bayesian/stochastic pyrometry, (6) Graphite coating method, (7) Virtual blackbody (wedge) method, (8) Neural network/AI approaches, (9) Thin-film optics/geometric optics modeling. Use the evidence gathered from papers by Suleiman 2025, Suleiman 2023, Giulietti 2025, Urban 2022, Ren 2019, Kieruj 2016, Lassila 2023, Grujic 2023, Narayanan 2023, Belikov 2024, Shi 2015, Cullinan 2025."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Method | Principle | Advantages | Limitations | Key References |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Blackbody reference calibration | Calibrate pyrometers/IR systems against a traceable blackbody or blackbody-equivalent source; instrument response factors are folded into calibration constants, and reflected/background radiation can be corrected if surroundings are characterized. | Traceable baseline; standard industrial practice; improves absolute accuracy; supports periodic recalibration and uncertainty control. | Does not solve process-time emissivity drift from oxidation/roughness; field conditions can differ from lab calibration; background reflections and alignment still matter. | (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000004) |
| Thermocouple cross-calibration | Adjust pyrometer emissivity or correction curves until non-contact readings match embedded or attached thermocouple temperatures under controlled conditions. | Simple and widely used; practical for alloy- and surface-specific emissivity tuning; useful for furnace and annealing trials. | Thermocouples perturb the specimen and may not represent true surface temperature during fast transients or induction heating; contact methods are difficult on moving/rotating parts. | (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000045) |
| Ratio / dual-wavelength pyrometry | Infer temperature from the ratio of radiances at two wavelengths so common factors partly cancel; accuracy depends on a stable or known emissivity ratio between the two bands. | Less sensitive than single-color pyrometry to absolute emissivity, attenuation, and partial field-of-view filling; fast and practical for dynamic heating. | Not truly emissivity-independent if emissivity ratio changes with oxide growth or wavelength spacing; errors increase when surfaces are non-gray or rapidly evolving. | (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000021) |
| Multi-wavelength pyrometry with polynomial emissivity models | Use 3+ wavelengths and fit temperature simultaneously with emissivity represented by linear/log-linear/polynomial/exponential wavelength functions, often by least squares. | Can compensate evolving wavelength dependence better than ratio pyrometry; can estimate both emissivity trend and temperature; suitable for complex metallic surfaces. | Sensitive to noise, model-form error, and overfitting; performance degrades when oxidation/roughness causes behavior outside the assumed emissivity model. | (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000001) |
| Bayesian / stochastic pyrometry | Treat emissivity, calibration coefficients, and noise probabilistically; infer posterior temperature with MAP estimate and credibility intervals using ex situ, in situ, or conditional emissivity priors. | Explicit uncertainty quantification; handles poorly known emissivity; adaptable to dew point, alloy, and process state; can outperform deterministic methods when emissivity varies strongly. | Strongly dependent on prior quality; wide intervals remain when emissivity is weakly constrained; computationally heavier and may still miss industrial accuracy targets. | (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000028) |
| Graphite coating method | Apply a thin high-emissivity coating with known spectral emissivity so radiometric temperature retrieval is less sensitive to the substrate’s unknown emissivity. | Effective way to impose a near-known emissivity; useful in induction-heated high-temperature metrology; simplifies inversion and uncertainty analysis. | Coating can degrade or spall at high temperature; may alter thermal behavior; not always acceptable for process samples or production parts. | (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033) |
| Virtual blackbody (wedge) method | Use a wedge/cavity geometry on the sample or fixture to create an effective emissivity near unity, providing a local blackbody-like reference for calibration or validation. | Reduces emissivity uncertainty without coating; useful for industrial strip/coil practice; provides an internal reference. | Sensitive to alignment and geometry; may be difficult to implement in compact or moving induction setups; only samples local geometry. | (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000001) |
| Neural network / AI approaches | Use multispectral data-driven inversion to recognize emissivity behavior and estimate temperature/emissivity without prescribing a fixed analytical emissivity model. | Can capture nonlinear emissivity behavior and automate model selection; promising for online monitoring and process control. | Requires representative training data; limited interpretability; robustness outside the training domain is uncertain. | (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000009) |
| Thin-film optics / geometric optics modeling | Model emissivity changes from oxide-film thickness, refractive index, roughness, nodules, and scattering using thin-film interference, geometric optics, or hybrid ray-tracing approaches. | Physically grounded correction path for annealing steels where oxide growth dominates emissivity; explains wavelength-dependent oscillations and atmosphere effects. | Requires optical constants, oxide morphology/thickness, and often process-specific validation; difficult to use as a standalone real-time correction. | (pqac-00000039, pqac-00000040, pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042) |


*Table: This table summarizes the main calibration and correction approaches used to manage emissivity-driven uncertainty in pyrometry during induction heating and annealing of metals. It compares how each method works, where it helps most, and its practical limitations.*

info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
query.title: Query

min_year.description:
filter for minimum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
min_year.title: Min Year

max_year.description:
filter for maximum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
max_year.title: Max Year

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility
        - OTHER: Other

    InterventionType:
        - BEHAVIORAL: Behavioral interventions
        - BIOLOGICAL: Biological interventions
        - COMBINATION_PRODUCT: Combination product interventions
        - DEVICE: Device interventions
        - DIAGNOSTIC_TEST: Diagnostic test interventions
        - DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: Dietary supplement interventions
        - DRUG: Drug interventions
        - GENETIC: Genetic interventions
        - PROCEDURE: Procedure interventions
        - RADIATION: Radiation interventions
        - OTHER: Other interventions

    DesignAllocation:
        - RANDOMIZED: Randomized allocation
        - NON_RANDOMIZED: Non-randomized allocation
        - NA: Not applicable

    InterventionalAssignment:
        - SINGLE_GROUP: Single group assignment
        - PARALLEL: Parallel assignment
        - CROSSOVER: Crossover assignment
        - FACTORIAL: Factorial assignment
        - SEQUENTIAL: Sequential assignment

    ObservationalModel:
        - COHORT: Cohort
        - CASE_CONTROL: Case-Control
        - CASE_ONLY: Case-Only
        - CASE_CROSSOVER: Case-Crossover
        - ECOLOGIC_OR_COMMUNITY: Ecologic or Community
        - FAMILY_BASED: Family-Based
        - DEFINED_POPULATION: Defined Population
        - NATURAL_HISTORY: Natural History
        - OTHER: Other

    DesignMasking:
        - NONE: None (Open Label)
        - SINGLE: Single
        - DOUBLE: Double
        - TRIPLE: Triple
        - QUADRUPLE: Quadruple

    WhoMasked:
        - PARTICIPANT: Participant
        - CARE_PROVIDER: Care Provider
        - INVESTIGATOR: Investigator
        - OUTCOMES_ASSESSOR: Outcomes Assessor
query.title: Query

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

answer.description: valid answer, cited with citation IDs.
answer.title: Answer

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: Review literature on emissivity and pyrometry uncertainty during induction heating/annealing of metallic samples (especially steel and nickel alloys). Summarize calibration/correction methods and provide references with bibtex.

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.

function.name: create_plan

messages.content:
Plan registered and formatted correctly, with some modifications:
{
  "objectives": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "objective": "Search for literature on emissivity and pyrometry uncertainty during induction heating of metals",
      "rationale": "Need to find relevant papers on the core topic of emissivity challenges and pyrometry uncertainty in induction heating/annealing contexts.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 2,
      "objective": "Search for calibration and correction methods for pyrometry in steel and nickel alloy processing",
      "rationale": "The user specifically asks about calibration/correction methods, so we need targeted searches for these techniques.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 3,
      "objective": "Search for emissivity measurement and modeling during annealing of steel",
      "rationale": "Annealing is specifically mentioned; emissivity changes during annealing (oxidation, phase transformation) are important.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 4,
      "objective": "Gather evidence from retrieved papers on emissivity behavior, pyrometry uncertainty, and correction methods",
      "rationale": "Need to deeply read the retrieved documents to extract specific findings, methods, and quantitative results.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 5,
      "objective": "Collect cited papers to find additional relevant references",
      "rationale": "Citation traversal may reveal seminal or complementary works not found in initial searches.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 6,
      "objective": "Create summary artifacts (table of methods, BibTeX references) and submit final answer",
      "rationale": "The user requests a summary of calibration/correction methods and BibTeX references.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    }
  ]
}

Abstract:
Experimental studies were carried out to determine the temperature and emissivity of 316L powder steel. It was found that the emissivity of powder steel varied from 0.33 to 0.58 in the temperature range from 59 °C to 900 °C. Based on the proposed methodology for quantitative assessment of temperature measurement uncertainty, it was established that the maximum value of the relative combined uncertainty in temperature measurement does not exceed 5.5%. Using the measure of agreement (normalized deviation), it was confirmed that the obtained results demonstrated the laboratory’s sufficient technical competence.

Abstract:
With increasing demand of thermophysical property data at high temperatures in the field e.g. of space aeronautics and manufacturing new measurement methods need to be established to guarantee reliable and SI-traceable measurements up to 3000 K. In the framework of the European Metrology Programme for Innovation and Research (EMPIR) within the joint research project Hi-Trace the dynamic emissivity measurement at PTB has been advanced to determine the specific heat up to around 2000 K. This paper outlines the necessary technical advancements such as an induction heating system, the measurement principle which utilize graphite coated samples and uncertainty evaluation using an adaptive Monte Carlo method. The specific heat of graphite and tungsten was measured in a temperature range from 1000 K to 2000 K with relative expanded uncertainties (k=2\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$k=2$$\end{document}) ranging from 8 % to 12 %.

Cylindrical samples made of nickel-based alloys Inconel 625, Inconel 718, Waspaloy and tungsten-carbides based on cobalt matrix were analyzed. The samples’ temperature in contact method was compared to the temperature measured by non-contact pyrometers. Based on this relative, the value of the emissivity coefficient was adjusted to the right indication of pyrometers.

BibTex:
@article{giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement,
    author = "Giulietti, Nicola and Cosoli, Gloria and Napolitano, Rachele and Pandarese, Giuseppe and Revel, Gian Marco and Chiariotti, Paolo",
    title = "Spectral emissivity measurement for high-temperature applications: a systematic review",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "Acta IMEKO",
    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846",
    volume = "14",
    pages = "1-17",
    publisher = "IMEKO International Measurement Confederation",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "2221-870X"
}


Abstract:
Measuring emissivity is pivotal for obtaining reliable high-temperature measurements through non-contact techniques such as pyrometry and thermal imaging. The interest in characterizing materials in terms of their radiant properties has increased in recent years due to the expanded application fields of these techniques, ranging from the process industry to aerospace and the energy industry. Various methods are available in the literature to assess material emissivity, but they are primarily classified into indirect and direct methods. This review also addresses different types of materials, various experimental conditions (e.g., heating technologies and measurement frequency bands), and different types of measurement outputs. The aim of this review paper is therefore to systematically examine the literature available on the topic, highlighting the pros and cons of the different methodologies used for measuring emissivity at temperatures up to 2500 °C.

## 3. Effect of Surface Oxidization on the Normal Spectral Emissivity of Straight Carbon Steel at 800–1100 K in Air
Valid Text Names:
'shi2015effectofsurface pages 1-2', 'shi2015effectofsurface pages 2-3', 'shi2015effectofsurface pages 3-5', 'shi2015effectofsurface pages 5-6', 'shi2015effectofsurface pages 6-7', 'shi2015effectofsurface pages 7-8', 'shi2015effectofsurface pages 8-9', 'shi2015effectofsurface pages 9-9'

BibTex:
@article{shi2015effectofsurface,
    author = "Shi, Deheng and Zou, Fenghui and Zhu, Zunlue and Sun, Jinfeng",
    title = "Effect of Surface Oxidization on the Normal Spectral Emissivity of Straight Carbon Steel at 800–1100 K in Air",
    year = "2015",
    journal = "Isij International",
    volume = "55",
    pages = "697-705",
    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.2355/isijinternational.55.697",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.2355/isijinternational.55.697",
    publisher = "Iron and Steel Institute of Japan",
    issue = "3",
    issn = "0915-1559"
}


Abstract:
This paper studies the effect of surface oxidization on the normal spectral emissivity of straight carbon steel Q235 in air at 1.5 μm over the temperature range from 800 to 1 100 K. For this reason, the normal spectral emissivity of straight carbon steel Q235 is measured at sixteen definite temperatures over a 6hour heating period. The normal radiance emitted from the specimen is received by an InGaAs photodiode detector. The temperature of specimen surface is measured by the two platinum-rhodium thermocouples. The variation of normal spectral emissivity with the heating time is studied at a given temperature. The variation of normal spectral emissivity with the temperature is evaluated at a definite heating time. The strong oscillation of normal spectral emissivity is discussed, which is affirmed to be connected with the thickness of oxide layer on the specimen surface, and originate from the interference effect between the radiation stemming from the oxide layer on the specimen surface and the radiation coming from the underlying metal substrate. The uncertainty of normal spectral emissivity contributed only by the surface oxidization is about 1.5–7.1%, and the uncertainty of temperature generated only by the surface oxidization is about 1.7–5.8 K. The models between the normal spectral emissivity and the heating time or temperature are evaluated. A simple functional form including the exponential and logarithmic functions can be used to reproduce well the variation of normal spectral emissivity with the heating time at a given temperature, including the reproduction of strong oscillations.

## 1. Fast Multi-Wavelength Pyrometer for Dynamic Temperature Measurements
Valid Text Names:
'belikov2024fastmultiwavelengthpyrometer pages 1-4', 'belikov2024fastmultiwavelengthpyrometer pages 4-8', 'belikov2024fastmultiwavelengthpyrometer pages 8-11', 'belikov2024fastmultiwavelengthpyrometer pages 11-12'

    title = "Fast Multi-Wavelength Pyrometer for Dynamic Temperature Measurements",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "International Journal of Thermophysics",
    volume = "45",
    pages = "1-12",
    month = "Feb",
    doi = "10.1007/s10765-023-03323-x",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1007/s10765-023-03323-x",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "2",
    issn = "0195-928X"
}


Abstract:
Multi-wavelength pyrometry is an efficient tool for measuring high temperatures in dynamic experiments. A fast 5-channel pyrometer was built and successfully employed in ion-beam heating experiments at the GSI Centre for Heavy Ion Research (Darmstadt, Germany). Temperatures of metallic samples heated by an intense focused heavy ion beam up to their melting points and beyond were measured with nanosecond time resolution and a spatial resolution of about 200 μm. The modular instrument has demonstrated its high versatility also for temperature measurements of exothermic reactions with millisecond temporal resolution.

Abstract:
Real-time closed-loop control of metallurgical processes is still in its infancy, mostly based on simple models and limited sensor data and challenged by extreme temperature and harsh process conditions. Contact-free thermal imaging-based measurement approaches thus appear to be particularly suitable for process monitoring. With the potential to generate vast amounts of accurate data in real time and combined with artificial intelligence methods to enable real-time analysis and integration of expert knowledge, thermal spectral imaging is identified as a promising method offering more robust and accurate identification of key parameters, such as surface temperature, morphology, composition, and flow rate.

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the main sources of emissivity uncertainty and pyrometry errors during induction heating and annealing of steel and nickel alloys? What calibration and correction methods are used to improve temperature measurement accuracy?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 63-66'.

FK Suleiman. Improving pyrometry of advanced high strength steels during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt identifies the main physical causes of emissivity variation during annealing/heat treatment: intrinsic wavelength- and temperature-dependence (Drude/Hagen-Rubens behaviour and a crossover/X point), surface topography/roughness (σ/λ effects and increased scattering), and the formation of surface oxides whose composition, morphology (thin film vs nodular), uniformity and thickness (reported 40–320 nm) change radiative properties. Thin oxide films (d ≤ λ) produce thin-film interference (constructive/destructive effects), whereas for λ >> d roughness and nodular oxides dominate via scattering. Selective oxidation (e.g., Mn, Al, Cr) and processing atmosphere (dew point) lead to composition-dependent emissivity changes. For calibration/correction, the text cites emissivity compensation algorithms: Hagen–Rubens-based compensation, linear and log-linear polynomial wavelength models, dual- and multi-wavelength (two-, three-, four-color) pyrometry and ratio/multicolor methods, and the use of constant-emissivity ratios across wavelengths. Limitations are noted: multi-wavelength algorithms are robust for uncontaminated surfaces but can be unreliable when emissivity is strongly affected by roughness or oxide formation. Modeling approaches such as thin-film optics and geometric optics approximation (GOA) ray-tracing are used to predict effects of films and roughness.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'narayanan2023aninvestigationinto pages 36-40'.

NS Narayanan. An investigation into radiative property variations across pre-annealed advanced high strength steel coils. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt identifies key sources of emissivity uncertainty and resulting pyrometry errors during annealing: emissivity dependence on temperature and surface state (including oxide formation), changes in emissivity ratios between wavelengths, and measurement geometry/misalignment (e.g., wedge virtual-blackbody sensitivity). Single-wavelength pyrometry requires accurate spectral emissivity and is vulnerable to emissivity changes during annealing. Dual-wavelength methods remove the need for absolute emissivity but require a known relationship (ratio) between spectral emissivities; deviations in emissivity ratios (for example from oxidation) produce temperature errors, while choosing wavelengths farther apart reduces error. Multi-wavelength methods use an emissivity polynomial and infer coefficients plus temperature; they can compensate evolving emissivity but are susceptible to over-fitting and noise unless a least-squares approach with sufficient wavelengths (n >= m+2) is used. Additional practical calibration/correction elements mentioned include using a wedge to create a virtual blackbody (emissivity ≈ 1), interpolating emissivity ratios from brightness-emissivity datasets (grey-body assumption), grouping instrument factors into a calibration constant, and validating pyrometers against thermocouples.

The excerpt summarizes dual-wavelength (ratio or two-color) and multi-wavelength pyrometry approaches relevant to high-temperature steel processing. Dual-wavelength methods estimate temperature from the ratio of spectral irradiances and are robust to atmospheric absorption and partially filled fields of view because “the ratio of the measured spectral irradiances cancels out these effects.” Accurate ratio pyrometry requires a known, temperature-independent emissivity ratio (“e-slope”); methods like TRACE build an experimentally derived reciprocal emissivity function to improve accuracy. Multi-wavelength (MW) pyrometry uses three or more wavelengths with emissivity compensation algorithms (commonly polynomial or exponential forms) to model wavelength- and temperature-dependent emissivity; coefficients are typically inferred simultaneously with temperature. MW solutions can be obtained by exact or least-squares methods, with objective functions in raw counts or log space (e.g., χ2 using ln(counts)). Literature also includes physics-based relations (Hagen-Rubens, Fresnel) for emissivity modelling. The excerpt notes MW methods are robust for uncontaminated surfaces but can fail when emissivity behavior is complex or evolving. The excerpt does not describe thermocouple cross-calibration, blackbody calibration procedures, Bayesian inference implementations, or neural-network approaches.

## pqac-00000015

From text 'kieruj2016determinationofemissivity pages 1-2'.

Piotr Kieruj, Damian Przestacki, and Tadeusz Chwalczuk. Determination of emissivity coefficient of heat-resistant super alloys and cemented carbide. Archives of Mechanical Technology and Materials, 36:30-34, Dec 2016. URL: https://doi.org/10.1515/amtm-2016-0006, doi:10.1515/amtm-2016-0006. This article has 33 citations.

The paper experimentally analyzed emissivity of difficult-to-machine materials—nickel-based superalloys Inconel 625, Inconel 718 and Waspaloy—and tungsten-carbide (WC-Co) layers. Temperatures measured by contact methods were compared with non-contact pyrometers, and the emissivity coefficient was adjusted from that comparison to obtain correct pyrometer indications. The authors note that pyrometers require a known emissivity and that a non-black body’s emissivity is always less than a black body’s. Pyrometer readings collect all radiation from the body and measurement deviations grow at lower temperatures. Emissivity depends strongly on material type and surface condition (roughness, cutting marks, reflectivity, color). The workpieces included laser-clad WC (about 3 mm, 11–13% Co, 5.2–5.6% C) on substrates; Waspaloy and Inconels are chromium-containing nickel alloys (Waspaloy having higher strength/stability). Because rotating workpieces limit sensor attachment, non-contact methods (pyrometry) are commonly used, necessitating emissivity correction based on contact/non-contact comparisons to improve temperature accuracy during laser-assisted machining.


---

## pqac-00000016

From text 'kieruj2016determinationofemissivity pages 2-4'.

Piotr Kieruj, Damian Przestacki, and Tadeusz Chwalczuk. Determination of emissivity coefficient of heat-resistant super alloys and cemented carbide. Archives of Mechanical Technology and Materials, 36:30-34, Dec 2016. URL: https://doi.org/10.1515/amtm-2016-0006, doi:10.1515/amtm-2016-0006. This article has 33 citations.

The study measured emissivity of Inconel 625, Inconel 718, Waspaloy and WC-Co using two infrared pyrometers (AHLBORN and RAYTEK) with different spectral ranges and optical resolutions. Samples were heated in a chamber furnace to uniform bulk temperature (to avoid surface-only heating) and thermocouples placed in blind holes (12 mm deep, closed ≈2 mm) provided reference temperatures. Measurements were taken outside the furnace (to avoid furnace radiation) with fixed pyrometer and sample geometry; for Inconel samples the distance to pyrometer no.4 was 600 mm. Emissivity was determined by adjusting the pyrometer emissivity setting until the pyrometer reading matched the thermocouple temperature (calibration/correction method). The paper emphasizes that emissivity depends on material, surface condition (roughness, cutting marks, reflectivity, color), temperature and measurement spectral range. Nickel-base alloys developed oxide matting with increasing temperature (changing emissivity), whereas WC-Co retained color up to >600°C. Pyrometers used allow emissivity settings 0.10–1.00. The work aimed to quantify temperature influence, material differences, and establish stable emissivity values across temperature ranges with minimal error.

## pqac-00000019

From text 'cunnington1973developmentoftechniques pages 56-61'.

GR Cunnington, AI Funaa, and PE Cassady. Development of techniques and associated instrumentation for high temperature emissivity measurements. Unknown journal, 1973.

The excerpt outlines pyrometry limits and methods relevant to measuring and correcting emissivity for high-temperature materials. Measured brightness temperature depends on the spectral emittance at the pyrometer wavelength, so single-color optical pyrometers require accurate spectral-emissivity values to avoid large temperature errors. If a material behaves as a gray body (wavelength-independent emittance), multi-band or two-color methods can remove the emittance term, but two-color pyrometry only helps when emittances at the selected bands are nearly equal. Numerical examples show that at 2000 K a surface assumed to have emissivity 0.5 must actually lie between 0.45–0.55 for 1% temperature accuracy; if assumed 0.8, a true spectral emissivity of 0.7–0.9 yields ~1% error. Polished metals can have spectral emissivity much higher than total emissivity, producing very large errors if spectral data are not available. Practical measurement approaches mentioned are using a cavity (small L/D=4 cavity with assumed unity effective emittance) as a true-temperature reference and combining an optical pyrometer with a total-radiation pyrometer to compute total normal emittance (claimed accuracy within ~5% under stated ratio conditions). These points imply that for nickel superalloys (Inconel, Waspaloy) you must measure spectral emissivity at the pyrometer wavelength, account for surface condition, and use cavity or combined-instrument techniques to correct pyrometric temperatures.

It cites experimental emissivity data for high-purity nickel between 1440 and 1605 K, studies of spectral emissivity for oxidized and roughened metal surfaces, and investigations into effects of surface roughness, oxidation, and temperature on emissivity. Several entries cover pyrometry methodology: wavelength selection and pyrometer type effects on measurement error, multispectral/multi-wavelength radiation thermometry (MRT) approaches, and a new apparatus for emissivity measurement. Modeling and interpretation approaches referenced include hybrid thin-film/geometric optics models for strip radiative properties and a Bayesian approach to pyrometry for evolving emissivity during annealing. Foundational texts on radiative properties of opaque materials and standards are also listed. Collectively these suggest measurement best practices for nickel superalloys: (1) expect strong dependence on oxide films, roughness, and temperature; (2) use multi-wavelength or multispectral methods with appropriate emissivity models or Bayesian/inverse methods to correct pyrometer readings; and (3) consult experimental nickel emissivity data and thin-film/oxide modeling for correction factors at high temperatures.

## pqac-00000021

From text 'giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 11-12'.

Nicola Giulietti, Gloria Cosoli, Rachele Napolitano, Giuseppe Pandarese, Gian Marco Revel, and Paolo Chiariotti. Spectral emissivity measurement for high-temperature applications: a systematic review. Acta IMEKO, 14:1-17, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846, doi:10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reviews high-temperature emissivity measurement methods and instruments relevant for pyrometry corrections. It highlights both contact and non-contact tools (e.g., pyrometer and IR camera) and notes that choice depends on their accuracy and trade-offs. Two-colour (ratio) pyrometry can make the voltage ratio a function of temperature and largely independent of emissivity if the wavelengths are sufficiently close. A narrow-bandwidth radiation pyrometer (1.45-1.8 µm) and two-colour pyrometers combined with FTIR and vacuum chambers have been used for high-temperature emissivity studies (example: stainless steel 316L powder at 977-1477 °C). The review contrasts indirect and direct methods: indirect approaches are better for low-emissivity materials and near-IR/VIS ranges, while direct methods (including calorimetry) measure overall hemispherical radiation across wavelengths; calorimetric apparatuses are described as conceptually simple and relatively low-cost. Practical issues noted include background reflections at lower temperatures, spectral-range limits of non-contact methods, need for thermal insulation and vacuum for some setups, and reported measurement errors on the order of ~1% in some two-colour comparisons.

## pqac-00000022

From text 'lassila2023effectofscalea pages 26-30'.

E Lassila. Effect of scale formation on the emissivity of austenitic stainless steels in an annealing furnace. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt defines emissivity as a surface property quantifying how much a surface radiates compared with a blackbody and notes it depends on wavelength and temperature. Spectral emissivity is obtained by comparing radiance from the surface to a blackbody at the same temperature; for radiation thermometers a normal spectral emissivity relation is provided (ɛ(λ,T) = [exp(λT)−1] / [exp(λTa)−1] in the excerpt). Emissivity measurement is performed by radiation thermometers (pyrometers) or spectrometers, and instruments allow an emissivity setting to improve temperature measurement accuracy. The text also emphasizes that surface scale/oxide formation affects the surface and thus emissivity: thin oxide layers (<20 nm) are modeled by Mott–Cabrera theory, and experimental work on stainless steels used kinetic models (parabolic, cubic, quadratic, and a 1.7 power model) to predict scale growth under different atmospheres and temperatures, with activation energies and frequency factors obtained via Arrhenius fits. These points link surface chemistry/oxide scale evolution to emissivity and to practical measurement considerations with radiation thermometry.

## pqac-00000023

From text 'suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 156-160'.

FK Suleiman. Improving pyrometry of advanced high strength steels during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt describes a Bayesian pyrometry framework that returns a MAP temperature estimate together with a credibility interval. While the Bayesian model accounts for uncertainties from spectral emissivity, calibration coefficients, and measurement noise, the dominant contributor to wide credibility intervals is the imperfectly known spectral emissivity. Reported uncertainties range from ±14 K to ±31 K depending on annealing dew point — far larger than industrial requirements (steelmakers desire ±2°C to ±4°C). Prior chapters demonstrate that in situ and ex situ emissivities differ substantially (leading to pyrometry absolute errors of tens of degrees and relative errors up to ~10%), driven by temperature-dependent surface changes and oxide formation during annealing. This motivates the work introduced in Chapter 9: a "Conditional emissivity prior" intended to improve temperature estimates by better capturing the temperature- and process-dependent evolution of spectral emissivity during intercritical annealing. The excerpt therefore highlights the problem (emissivity-dominated uncertainty), empirical evidence (uncertainty magnitudes and ex situ vs in situ discrepancies), and the proposed direction (conditional emissivity prior) to reduce Bayesian pyrometry uncertainty.


---

## pqac-00000024

From text 'suleiman2023effectofdew pages 7-9'.

FK Suleiman, NS Narayanan, and KJ Daun. Effect of dew point on the evolving spectral emissivity of advanced high strength steel during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2023.

The paper implements a Bayesian pyrometry approach that models spectral emissivity with stochastic priors (ex situ and in situ) described as normal distributions (mean με and standard deviation σε). Because normal priors concentrate probability near the mean (με ± 3σε covers ~99.7% of observations), true emissivity values in low-probability regions produce biased temperature estimates. The in situ-based prior (model #4) delivers more reliable and robust peak annealing temperature (PAT) estimates across dewpoints and yields a ±21°C credibility interval that compensates for emissivity variability, whereas the ex situ prior can fail when its probability density is low (e.g., −30°C) or when sample size/representativeness is limited. The in situ prior may also underestimate variance at low temperatures because it was built from 520–800°C data. Emissivity trends: it increases with dewpoint (notably below 2 μm) and decreases with temperature above 400°C, affecting pyrometric bias (all models underpredict soak at −30°C and overpredict at other dewpoints). The authors recommend conditional/stage-specific stochastic priors (different priors for heating vs soaking) and combining ex situ and in situ priors to balance robustness and precision, highlighting that Bayesian methods provide uncertainty quantification unavailable from deterministic algorithms.


---

## pqac-00000025

From text 'suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 160-163'.

FK Suleiman. Improving pyrometry of advanced high strength steels during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt describes a Bayesian multi-wavelength pyrometry approach for steel annealing where uncertainty in spectral emissivity is the dominant source of temperature posterior uncertainty. Using an ex situ emissivity prior yields large temperature uncertainties (±15°C to ±31°C at 95% credibility) and MAP errors (±3°C to ±74°C), which fail to meet control targets of ±2–4°C. To improve accuracy and precision, the chapter proposes a conditional emissivity prior that conditions emissivity on annealing dew point (Tdp) and the equivalent blackbody temperature vector (Tbe). Because Tbe is computed from time-resolved spectral intensity assuming ε=1, the prior can adapt to evolving surface states and selective oxidation during annealing, increasing information content (narrower prior width) while retaining flexibility. The Bayesian model frames p(T, ε, K|S) via Bayes’ rule, models likelihood using Wien’s approximation to Planck’s law, and assumes independent Gaussian priors for T, ε, and K so the posterior can be approximated as Gaussian. The approach aims to reduce posterior width and improve MAP temperature estimates for AHSS annealing control.

## pqac-00000026

From text 'suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 156-160'.

FK Suleiman. Improving pyrometry of advanced high strength steels during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt shows that in situ spectral emissivity is critical for accurate pyrometry of DP980 AHSS: using ex situ emissivities can produce substantial temperature errors (relative errors up to ~10%). Pyrometry here uses three detection wavelengths (1.6, 2.1 and 2.4 μm) and a Wien-approximation formulation with constants C1 = 1.191×108 W·µm4/m2sr and C2 = 1.439 µm·K, solved by least-squares across wavelengths. Instrument detection thresholds (pyrometer 250°C, spectrometer 400°C) limit measurements. Emissivity is strongly temperature dependent: it peaks near 300–400°C and changes markedly between 650–750°C (possible Mn oxide transformations), so temperature-dependent emissivity models are necessary. The thesis employs a Bayesian pyrometry model (MAP estimate plus credibility interval), but uncertainty is currently dominated by uncertain emissivity, yielding estimated uncertainty ±14 K to ±31 K depending on dew point—far larger than industrial targets (±2–4°C). Chapter 9 proposes a "Conditional emissivity prior" to improve temperature estimates by better constraining emissivity within the Bayesian framework.

## pqac-00000027

From text 'suleiman2023effectofdewa pages 7-9'.

FK Suleiman, NS Narayanan, and KJ Daun. Effect of dew point on the evolving spectral emissivity of advanced high strength steel during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2023.

The paper implements a Bayesian pyrometry approach by modeling spectral emissivity with stochastic (normal) priors defined by a mean (με) and standard deviation (σε). Because normal priors concentrate 99.7% of mass within με ± 3σε, emissivity values that fall in low-probability tails produce biased temperature posteriors—Bayes assigns very low probability to those emissivities and the inferred temperature can under- or over-predict the true value. An in situ-based prior (model #4) built from high-temperature measurements gave more robust PAT estimates across dewpoints and provided a ±21°C credibility interval that compensates for emissivity variation; however, it underestimates variance at low temperatures because its training range was 520–800°C. An ex situ prior (model #3) had some strengths (better ramp-period estimates) but failed at certain dewpoints, possibly due to small sample size. Emissivity systematically increases with dewpoint and decreases above ~400°C, affecting pyrometry bias. The authors recommend designing statistically relevant priors (including different priors for heating vs soaking), combining ex situ and in situ priors, and reporting posterior uncertainty so steelmakers can compare estimates against temperature tolerances during intercritical annealing.

## pqac-00000028

From text 'suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 111-115'.

FK Suleiman. Improving pyrometry of advanced high strength steels during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt describes a Bayesian multispectral pyrometry approach for three-wavelength measurements where temperature T is the quantity of interest and the nuisance parameters φ combine spectral emissivities ε and calibration constants K. Measurement noise is initially framed by a Poisson–Gaussian model that leads to a Gaussian likelihood with covariance ΓS (simple form ΓS = diag(S̅λ)+σG), and the likelihood uses LS = chol(ΓS-1) with m=3 measurements. However, empirical characterization using a blackbody (350–850°C, 1160 shots per temperature) shows nonlinear variance vs. mean behavior; the noise is better fit by a sigmoid variance model (coefficients given in Table 6.1) rather than the linear Poisson–Gaussian assumption. Because three measurements cannot uniquely determine T and the emissivities, uninformed priors alone are inadequate; prior information is required. The work adopts an uninformed prior for T (ppr(T)=1) and multivariate normal (MVN) priors for K and ε (maximum-entropy given mean/covariance). A sample-based emissivity prior is constructed by fitting an MVN to 27 ex situ spectrometer measurements on cold-rolled DP980, and the framework treats all unknowns probabilistically via Bayes' equation, enabling stochastic estimation (posterior inference) of T accounting for emissivity uncertainty.
