## pqac-00000029

From text 'suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 85-89'.

FK Suleiman. Improving pyrometry of advanced high strength steels during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt highlights that spectral emissivity of AHSS during annealing is strongly wavelength- and temperature-dependent, with metals showing a crossover wavelength where emissivity-temperature sensitivity reverses, and that surface oxidation often dominates radiative properties at shorter wavelengths. Because oxidation and temperature-dependent electrical conductivity alter emissivity, ex situ (room-temperature) measurements are insufficient; "additional in situ temperature-dependent emittance measurements will be required" to validate models for high-temperature pyrometry. The author used response surface methodology (RSM) to model emissivity as a function of dew point, Si/Mn ratio, pre-annealed surface state, and wavelength (1.2–4.0 μm; specifically 1.6, 2.1, 2.4 μm in a factorial design). ANOVA found Si/Mn ratio, surface state, and interactions (AB, BC) statistically significant, collectively explaining >=93% of variability at selected wavelengths. A linear regression model with main effects and multiple interactions was fitted, but residuals indicate missing second-order effects, suggesting the need for higher-order experimental designs. The text also documents the design requirements and features of an in situ annealing simulator (optical access, controlled atmosphere, PID temperature control, DC heating) necessary to obtain reliable emissivity and irradiance data for improved pyrometry.


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 111-115'.

FK Suleiman. Improving pyrometry of advanced high strength steels during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt describes a Bayesian approach for three-wavelength pyrometry where temperature T is the quantity of interest and spectral emissivities ε and calibration constants K are treated as nuisance parameters. Measurement noise is modelled starting from a Poisson-Gaussian formulation and approximated as a heteroskedastic Gaussian; the standard linear variance model σ^2_si = S̅λi + σ_G^2 is given but experimental data showed strong nonlinearity. The noise was better captured by a sigmoid noise profile (coefficients reported in Table 6.1). Because the number of unknowns exceeds measurements, priors are required: an uninformative prior is used for T (ppr(T)=1), while MVN priors are specified for K and ε. A sample-based emissivity prior was constructed by fitting an MVN to 27 ex situ spectrometer measurements on cold-rolled DP980 coupons. The likelihood is Gaussian (using the measurement covariance ΓS) with Smeas and Smod(T,φ) defined, and the posterior p(T,φ|S) ∝ p(S|T,φ)ppr(T)ppr(φ). These elements (noise model, MVN emissivity prior, and Gaussian likelihood) form the basis for stochastic Bayesian estimation of surface temperature under emissivity uncertainty.

## pqac-00000031

From text 'urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 1-4'.

David Urban and Klaus Anhalt. Dynamic measurement of specific heat above 1000 k. International Journal of Thermophysics, Mar 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10765-022-03005-0, doi:10.1007/s10765-022-03005-0. This article has 1 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The PTB method uses a modified laser-flash apparatus for dynamic emissivity and specific-heat determination up to ~2000 K. Samples are brought to equilibrium and a short ND:YAG laser pulse (~1 ms, ~1 J) heats the front face; the resulting 1–3 K back-face adiabatic temperature rise is measured with an absolutely calibrated radiation thermometer. The direct measurands are the incident laser energy and the adiabatic temperature rise; Planck-law radiances and transmissivity are used in a heat-transfer model that includes radiation and conduction and the laser pulse shape. Kirchhoff’s law (at λ0 = 1064 nm) links emissivity and absorption. If spectral emissivity is known, the system of equations is solved for specific heat; alternatively, applying a thin graphite coating (emissivity ελ = ελ,C) provides a known/high emissivity to enable cp extraction. To reduce stray/background radiation and improve measurement uncertainty, PTB implemented a dedicated inductive heating system and a new chamber that heats primarily the sample. The paper also reports measurements for graphite and tungsten from 1000–2000 K with expanded uncertainties (k=2) of 8–12% and mentions uncertainty evaluation using an adaptive Monte Carlo method.


---

## pqac-00000032

From text 'urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 4-8'.

David Urban and Klaus Anhalt. Dynamic measurement of specific heat above 1000 k. International Journal of Thermophysics, Mar 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10765-022-03005-0, doi:10.1007/s10765-022-03005-0. This article has 1 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The study describes an induction-heating based setup for dynamic emissivity and high-temperature specific-heat measurements. Heating is provided by an Ambrell EASYHEAT induction system (max. 6 kW, 240 kHz) with a water-cooled coil/generator and three linear positioning stages to optimize coupling; controlled heating rates of 3–5 K/s are used and temperature drift is reported (ΔT1250K < 150 mK/min, ΔT2250K < 500 mK/min). The sample chamber has v-grooved, Nextel-painted walls to reduce reflections. Samples are disc-shaped (≈3 mm height, 10 mm diameter; contact minimized by five small points) and sit in an isostatically pressed graphite holder; an aperture with water cooling (~15 °C) confines the laser profile. For known emissivity, surfaces are prepared by polishing (280 grit), ultrasonic cleaning (15 min in ethanol), and coating with a thin graphite spray (Kontakt Chemie Graphit 33) that is hardened above 100 °C to leave a thin layer. A coated tungsten sample gained ~0.0005 g of coating (total mass 4.370 g), and the graphite coating emissivity at 1064 nm was determined as ελ,C = 0.97 ± 0.03 below 2250 K. A graphite susceptor ring can be used to assist heating of poorly inductive samples. The measurement principle can be inverted to obtain specific heat if spectral emissivity at 1064 nm is known.


---

## pqac-00000033

From text 'urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 14-16'.

David Urban and Klaus Anhalt. Dynamic measurement of specific heat above 1000 k. International Journal of Thermophysics, Mar 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10765-022-03005-0, doi:10.1007/s10765-022-03005-0. This article has 1 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes a dynamic high-temperature measurement method using induction heating with samples prepared by applying a graphite spray coating. The authors report that uncertainty in the measured specific heat is dominated by the coating emissivity (ucp(ελ,C) ≈ 80%), and measurements with damaged coatings were excluded. The induction-heating approach allows potentially measuring specific heat at multiple temperatures per cycle. Practical limits are noted: the graphite spray becomes fragile near ≈2000 K, restricting the usable temperature range and motivating investigation of coating durability versus temperature, duration, and substrate. Alternative coating methods (e.g., sputtering or painting) are suggested to produce thicker, more resistant coatings, though changes to thermophysical properties must be considered. The Hi-Trace project will characterize reference materials (graphite, tungsten, molybdenum) for specific heat and emissivity; using reference samples with known specific heat or known emissivity could enable dynamic emissivity measurement, reduce emissivity uncertainty, and include temperature dependence, thereby improving dynamic specific-heat measurements.

## pqac-00000034

From text 'giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 9-10'.

Nicola Giulietti, Gloria Cosoli, Rachele Napolitano, Giuseppe Pandarese, Gian Marco Revel, and Paolo Chiariotti. Spectral emissivity measurement for high-temperature applications: a systematic review. Acta IMEKO, 14:1-17, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846, doi:10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt surveys high-temperature emissivity measurement methods and provides information applicable to induction-heating experiments and dynamic emissivity measurement. Using a vacuum chamber reduces uncertainties (<1%) and limits oxidation and atmospheric radiation, which is important for high-temperature samples (e.g., coatings that may oxidize). Combined measurement systems can obtain normal spectral emissivity, optical constants, and thickness simultaneously; a thermopile with a Christiansen effect filter helps define a zero-reflectivity wavelength for accurate surface temperature and emissivity. Improved calibration (modified two-temperature calibration) yielded a combined uncertainty below 2.6% at 1200 °C on SiC. Transient/dynamic emissivity has been measured using a spectrometer synchronized with a high-speed camera (transient spectral emissivity of a thermocouple), and multi-wavelength radiation thermometry with fibre-optic spectrometers has been used in-situ up to ~1250 °C. Thermography with calibrated blackbodies or thermocouples is common (reported accuracy 0.005 for emissivity ≥0.92 in one setup), while modulated IR sources enable emissivity estimation without an external reference. Inverse methods (e.g., SPSO) can retrieve directional-spectral emissivity from measured optical properties. These approaches and precautions (vacuum, calibration, multi-wavelength/inverse methods) are directly relevant when measuring dynamic emissivity during induction heating or evaluating high-temp coatings like graphite.

## pqac-00000035

Nicola Giulietti, Gloria Cosoli, Rachele Napolitano, Giuseppe Pandarese, Gian Marco Revel, and Paolo Chiariotti. URL: https://doi.org/10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846, doi:10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reviews direct emissivity measurement systems relevant to high-temperature applications and contains points applicable to induction-heating and dynamic measurements. Some experimental setups employ eddy-current heaters (relevant to induction heating) together with IR cameras and K-type thermocouples for synchronous radiometric and contact temperature readings. Measurement challenges under transient or non-uniform heating are highlighted: McDaid proposed an iterative method showing that slight changes in temperature distribution can cause large variations in radiative heat loss, which is critical for dynamic/induction heating. Angular dependence is important (emissivity changes above ~75° viewing angle). A recent in-situ thermal-imager approach (with a blackbody-equivalent verification) achieved an emissivity uncertainty of about 0.05 over ~313–823 K, demonstrating feasibility of online emissivity estimation up to ~500 °C. Material-dependent behavior is noted (example: tungsten emissivity increases with temperature but decreases with wavelength). The table also indicates use of vacuum vessels and multi-sensor layouts (video cameras, thermocouples) in some high-temperature emissivity experiments.

## pqac-00000036

From text 'giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 8-9'.

Nicola Giulietti, Gloria Cosoli, Rachele Napolitano, Giuseppe Pandarese, Gian Marco Revel, and Paolo Chiariotti. Spectral emissivity measurement for high-temperature applications: a systematic review. Acta IMEKO, 14:1-17, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846, doi:10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt summarizes experimental approaches and practical issues relevant to high-temperature and transient emissivity measurements. It reports water-cooled chambers and SiC heaters with a 'black hole' used to heat blackbodies up to 1500 °C with emissivity uncertainty below ~3%, while vacuum chambers can reduce uncertainties to <1% and limit oxidation and parasitic atmospheric radiation. Researchers use laser and CO2 laser heating to reach very high temperatures and short processing times (≤1 s), but non-uniform heating produces high thermal gradients; splitting a CO2 laser beam or using isothermal furnaces improves uniformity. Dynamic/transient measurements require fast instrumentation (high-speed spectrometers, short optical path lengths) and careful calibration to mitigate parasitic radiance. Simultaneous measurement of temperature and emissivity has been done with multiwavelength pyrometers and combined deterministic/Bayesian methods, but these require explicit temperature–emissivity separation considerations. The excerpt emphasizes use of blackbody references, controlled environments (vacuum or inert), and high temporal-resolution methods for dynamic emissivity tests. It does not discuss induction heating setups or graphite-coating emissivity methods specifically.

## pqac-00000037

From text 'giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 3-4'.

Nicola Giulietti, Gloria Cosoli, Rachele Napolitano, Giuseppe Pandarese, Gian Marco Revel, and Paolo Chiariotti. Spectral emissivity measurement for high-temperature applications: a systematic review. Acta IMEKO, 14:1-17, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846, doi:10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reviews factors and methods relevant to high-temperature spectral emissivity measurement. Key material factors include oxidation at high temperatures and surface roughness: both tend to increase emissivity, while specific coatings (e.g., carbonyl iron powders) can show emissivity decreases after annealing. Film thickness affects heat-resistance properties, and conductive layers often show increasing emissivity with temperature while emissivity generally decreases with increasing wavelength. Measurement methodologies are clustered into indirect and direct approaches; indirect methods commonly derive emissivity from reflectivity/transmissivity using Kirchhoff's law. Directional hemispherical reflectivity measured in an integrating sphere is a frequent indirect technique: samples are illuminated inside the sphere (incidence ~8–12°) and the system design, sphere coating, and non-ideal geometry can introduce measurement errors. The reviewed instrumentation covers high temperatures (examples up to ~327–1127 °C) and broad spectral ranges (e.g., 1–20 µm), indicating that established laboratory indirect systems can handle high-temperature, mid- to far-IR emissivity characterization.

## pqac-00000038

From text 'giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 2-3'.

Nicola Giulietti, Gloria Cosoli, Rachele Napolitano, Giuseppe Pandarese, Gian Marco Revel, and Paolo Chiariotti. Spectral emissivity measurement for high-temperature applications: a systematic review. Acta IMEKO, 14:1-17, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846, doi:10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt summarizes basic radiative properties and measurement approaches relevant to high-temperature emissivity characterization. Energy balance is expressed as α+γ+τ=1; for opaque materials (τ=0) Kirchhoff’s law gives α=ε, so emissivity can be related to reflectivity/transmissivity. Emissivity depends strongly on surface treatment and temperature; measurement approaches are grouped into indirect and direct methods, with indirect methods determining emissivity via reflectivity and transmissivity using Kirchhoff’s law. Two classes of reference materials are used: high-emissivity coatings (often non-conductive ceramics or inorganic composites) applied to cavities or metal surfaces to act as blackbodies and improve temperature measurement, and low-emissivity, highly reflective materials (often metals/alloys) used to reduce infrared emission. Optical properties change at high temperatures mainly due to oxidation and surface roughness—both tending to increase emissivity—and some spectral variations appear beyond ~20 μm. While the excerpt provides useful fundamentals (energy balance, use of coatings, indirect measurement principle and factors affecting emissivity), it does not detail induction heating measurement setups, graphite-coating-specific protocols, or dynamic emissivity measurement with induction systems.

## pqac-00000039

From text 'suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 63-66'.

FK Suleiman. Improving pyrometry of advanced high strength steels during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2025.

During intercritical annealing of advanced high-strength steels (AHSS), selective oxide formation (composition, morphology, thickness) substantially alters spectral emissivity. Oxide layers are typically thin (40–320 nm) and often dielectric/non-absorbing; when the film thickness is smaller than or comparable to the pyrometry wavelength (d ≤ λ) thin-film interference occurs: reflections from the film interfaces interfere constructively or destructively, producing wavelength-dependent oscillations in emissivity. The effect is stronger for uniform films with lower refractive index and weaker when the surface is discontinuous or nodular. For wavelengths much larger than the oxide thickness (λ >> d), surface roughness and oxide nodules dominate, increasing emissivity via multiple scattering. Emissivity also depends on temperature (Drude/Hagen-Rubens behavior for metals) and on process variables (alloy composition, annealing atmosphere/dew point) that control oxide growth; experiments show oxide growth can increase emissivity and that thinner oxides can give lower emissivity. These mechanisms explain observed spectral emissivity oscillations during oxide-scale growth and complicate pyrometric temperature measurement unless the oxide state is accounted for.

The excerpt explains how oxide scale growth during annealing strongly modifies spectral emissivity of advanced high-strength steels (AHSS). Oxide layers are thin (40–320 nm) and can form as uniform film-like layers or grainy/nodular morphologies; both may coexist and alter EM interactions differently. When film thickness is on the order of or smaller than the pyrometry wavelength (d ≤ λ), thin-film interference occurs: reflections from the film interfaces are phase-shifted producing constructive and destructive interference that modulates (oscillates) spectral emissivity with wavelength and film thickness. A lower refractive index of a uniform oxide film increases interference effects and raises spectral emissivity. At wavelengths much larger than the oxide thickness (λ >> d), surface roughness and oxide nodules dominate, causing random scattering, multiple reflections and generally increased emissivity. Pure-metal temperature trends (Drude/Hagen-Rubens) are complicated by oxide composition, morphology, thickness, atmosphere (dew point) and alloying (selective Mn/Al oxidation), so emissivity can vary during annealing cycles; thinner oxides have been associated with lower emissivity. These mechanisms explain oscillatory spectral emissivity behavior as oxide scales grow and change morphology during annealing.

The excerpt reports that during intercritical annealing of DP980 steel, the evolving oxide scale strongly affects spectral emissivity. When oxide-film thickness is comparable to the detection wavelength, thin-film interference produces wavelength-dependent features in the measured emissivity (most pronounced at shorter wavelengths). The presence of metallic oxides (continuous films or nodules) alters surface state and emissivity; selective oxidation (segregation of Si, Mn, Cr, etc.) is thermodynamically favored under the tested dewpoints, and the resulting oxide properties depend on oxidation thermodynamics and kinetics. With increasing temperature above ~400°C the spectral emissivity decreases at all dewpoints (potentially due to the “crossover wavelength” or X-point phenomenon and temperature-dependent oxide behavior). At high peak annealing temperature (800°C) some samples (lowest dewpoint) show emissivity returning toward pre-annealed values for wavelengths >2 µm, suggesting oxide reduction or thinning. function.arguments: {"question": "What are the quantitative uncertainties reported for pyrometry temperature measurements during steel annealing and induction heating? What temperature errors result from emissivity uncertainty?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000047

From text 'suleiman2023effectofdewa pages 7-9'.

FK Suleiman, NS Narayanan, and KJ Daun. Effect of dew point on the evolving spectral emissivity of advanced high strength steel during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt reports a Bayesian (stochastic) treatment of emissivity uncertainty for pyrometric temperature estimation during intercritical annealing. The only explicit quantitative uncertainty reported is a credibility interval of ±21°C for the peak annealing temperature (PAT) when using the in situ-based stochastic prior; this interval is presented as compensating for emissivity variations with dewpoint and temperature. Emissivity priors are modeled as Gaussian distributions (99.7% within με ± 3σε), so values in low-probability tails lead to poor temperature estimates. The authors note systematic biases: all models underpredict soak temperature at −30°C and overpredict at the other dewpoints; whether an estimate under- or overpredicts depends on whether the prior mean is greater or less than the actual emissivity. Spectral emissivity trends reported (increased with dewpoint, decreased with temperature above 400°C, effects stronger below 2 μm and at −30°C) are cited as causes of pyrometry errors. No other specific numeric temperature-error magnitudes from emissivity uncertainty (besides ±21°C) are provided in the excerpt.


---

## pqac-00000048

From text 'suleiman2023effectofdew pages 7-9'.

FK Suleiman, NS Narayanan, and KJ Daun. Effect of dew point on the evolving spectral emissivity of advanced high strength steel during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt reports a quantified uncertainty for pyrometric peak annealing temperature (PAT) estimates: an in situ–based stochastic emissivity prior produced a credibility interval of ±21°C for the PAT. It also states that all models underpredict soak temperature at a −30°C dewpoint and overpredict at the other dewpoints, though no other explicit numeric temperature errors are given. Emissivity was modeled as a normal distribution (με ± σε), with 99.7% of observations expected within με ± 3σε; when actual emissivity lies in low-probability tails the Bayesian posterior assigns very low probability to those emissivity values, producing under- or overpredicted temperatures. The in situ prior underestimated emissivity variance at lower temperatures because its training range was 520–800°C; the ex situ prior (27 samples) also failed at −30°C, suggesting limited sample size or poor representation of in situ variation. Spectral emissivity increases with dewpoint (notably below 2 μm) and decreases with temperature above ~400°C, effects that drive the emissivity-related temperature errors. The excerpt does not discuss induction heating or provide additional quantitative temperature-error magnitudes beyond the ±21°C credibility interval.


---

## pqac-00000049

From text 'suleiman2023effectofdew pages 5-7'.

FK Suleiman, NS Narayanan, and KJ Daun. Effect of dew point on the evolving spectral emissivity of advanced high strength steel during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt describes how emissivity uncertainty is treated for multi-wavelength pyrometry during intercritical annealing of DP980 steel. Deterministic compensation algorithms give a single inferred temperature, while Bayesian (stochastic) models represent spectral emissivity as a probability density function and thus return a temperature pdf whose width reflects uncertainty. The study evaluated four models: two deterministic algorithms and two Gaussian priors (model #3 ex situ, model #4 in situ). Bayesian results report 95% credibility intervals (stated as corresponding to two standard deviations) and note that Gaussian priors imply 99.7% of observations within ±3σ. Temperature estimation used three wavelengths (1.6, 2.1, 2.4 μm). Qualitatively, steady-state estimates at 800°C increase with dewpoint (emissivity interpreted as higher temperature); the ex situ prior (model #3) underpredicts peak annealing temperatures, and most models overpredict during the heating ramp. The excerpt does not provide explicit numeric temperature uncertainties (°C) or concrete temperature-error magnitudes arising from emissivity uncertainty on these pages.


---

## pqac-00000050

From text 'suleiman2023effectofdewa pages 5-7'.

FK Suleiman, NS Narayanan, and KJ Daun. Effect of dew point on the evolving spectral emissivity of advanced high strength steel during intercritical annealing. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt describes how pyrometric temperature uncertainty during intercritical annealing of AHSS is treated: deterministic multi-wavelength (MW) compensation algorithms produce a single temperature (no uncertainty), whereas Bayesian models represent spectral emissivity as a probability distribution, producing a temperature pdf (MAP plus credibility intervals). Two stochastic emissivity priors are compared: an ex situ prior from 27 spectrometer measurements (model #3) and an in situ prior from 443 measurements (model #4). Temperatures were inferred using intensities at 1.6, 2.1 and 2.4 μm. The Bayesian results include 95% credibility intervals (stated as corresponding to two standard deviations) and the text notes the statistical property that ~99.7% of observations lie within ±3σ of the mean. Emissivity uncertainty leads to systematic temperature errors: e.g., steady-state estimates at 800°C are overpredicted with increasing dewpoint (oxide-enhanced emissivity misinterpreted as higher temperature), and several models overpredict during the heating ramp; model #3 showed the worst underprediction at −30°C. The excerpt does not report explicit numeric temperature-uncertainty magnitudes (°C) or specific temperature-error values resulting from emissivity uncertainty.


---

## pqac-00000051

From text 'giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 11-11'.

Nicola Giulietti, Gloria Cosoli, Rachele Napolitano, Giuseppe Pandarese, Gian Marco Revel, and Paolo Chiariotti. Spectral emissivity measurement for high-temperature applications: a systematic review. Acta IMEKO, 14:1-17, Mar 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846, doi:10.21014/actaimeko.v14i1.1846. This article has 13 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt provides measured uncertainty figures for pyrometry and radiation-thermometer based temperature measurements drawn from Table 5. Reported relative uncertainties and errors span the single-percent range: examples include a "Relative uncertainty in temperature < 2.5 %", specific pyrometers with "< 3 % (at 657 °C - IGAR12-LO)" and "< 3 % (at 462 °C - M76S)", and one device showing "≈ 0 % (M67S)". Two-colour and simulation-comparison entries cite "Approximately 1 % error between simulated and measured temperature" and an "Absolute temperature error < 2 % at 727 °C". General instrument performance notes state "Repeatability (1 %) and accuracy (< 2 %)" and a listed "Arithmetic error < 1 %" for another case. The table includes measurements on stainless steel and stainless steel 316L powder, indicating relevance to steel annealing/processing studies. The excerpt does not explicitly convert emissivity uncertainty into a direct temperature-error formula, but the listed pyrometry uncertainties (roughly 0–3%) represent overall measurement errors that will include emissivity-related contributions; specific temperature error due solely to an assumed emissivity change is not given in the provided text.

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a summary table of calibration/correction methods for pyrometry during induction heating and annealing of metals. Columns: Method, Principle, Advantages, Limitations, Key References. Rows should cover: (1) Blackbody reference calibration, (2) Thermocouple cross-calibration, (3) Ratio/dual-wavelength pyrometry, (4) Multi-wavelength pyrometry with polynomial emissivity models, (5) Bayesian/stochastic pyrometry, (6) Graphite coating method, (7) Virtual blackbody (wedge) method, (8) Neural network/AI approaches, (9) Thin-film optics/geometric optics modeling. Use the evidence gathered from papers by Suleiman 2025, Suleiman 2023, Giulietti 2025, Urban 2022, Ren 2019, Kieruj 2016, Lassila 2023, Grujic 2023, Narayanan 2023, Belikov 2024, Shi 2015, Cullinan 2025."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Method | Principle | Advantages | Limitations | Key References |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Blackbody reference calibration | Calibrate pyrometers/IR systems against a traceable blackbody or blackbody-equivalent source; instrument response factors are folded into calibration constants, and reflected/background radiation can be corrected if surroundings are characterized. | Traceable baseline; standard industrial practice; improves absolute accuracy; supports periodic recalibration and uncertainty control. | Does not solve process-time emissivity drift from oxidation/roughness; field conditions can differ from lab calibration; background reflections and alignment still matter. | (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000004) |
| Thermocouple cross-calibration | Adjust pyrometer emissivity or correction curves until non-contact readings match embedded or attached thermocouple temperatures under controlled conditions. | Simple and widely used; practical for alloy- and surface-specific emissivity tuning; useful for furnace and annealing trials. | Thermocouples perturb the specimen and may not represent true surface temperature during fast transients or induction heating; contact methods are difficult on moving/rotating parts. | (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000045) |
| Ratio / dual-wavelength pyrometry | Infer temperature from the ratio of radiances at two wavelengths so common factors partly cancel; accuracy depends on a stable or known emissivity ratio between the two bands. | Less sensitive than single-color pyrometry to absolute emissivity, attenuation, and partial field-of-view filling; fast and practical for dynamic heating. | Not truly emissivity-independent if emissivity ratio changes with oxide growth or wavelength spacing; errors increase when surfaces are non-gray or rapidly evolving. | (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000021) |
| Multi-wavelength pyrometry with polynomial emissivity models | Use 3+ wavelengths and fit temperature simultaneously with emissivity represented by linear/log-linear/polynomial/exponential wavelength functions, often by least squares. | Can compensate evolving wavelength dependence better than ratio pyrometry; can estimate both emissivity trend and temperature; suitable for complex metallic surfaces. | Sensitive to noise, model-form error, and overfitting; performance degrades when oxidation/roughness causes behavior outside the assumed emissivity model. | (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000001) |
| Bayesian / stochastic pyrometry | Treat emissivity, calibration coefficients, and noise probabilistically; infer posterior temperature with MAP estimate and credibility intervals using ex situ, in situ, or conditional emissivity priors. | Explicit uncertainty quantification; handles poorly known emissivity; adaptable to dew point, alloy, and process state; can outperform deterministic methods when emissivity varies strongly. | Strongly dependent on prior quality; wide intervals remain when emissivity is weakly constrained; computationally heavier and may still miss industrial accuracy targets. | (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000028) |
| Graphite coating method | Apply a thin high-emissivity coating with known spectral emissivity so radiometric temperature retrieval is less sensitive to the substrate’s unknown emissivity. | Effective way to impose a near-known emissivity; useful in induction-heated high-temperature metrology; simplifies inversion and uncertainty analysis. | Coating can degrade or spall at high temperature; may alter thermal behavior; not always acceptable for process samples or production parts. | (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033) |
| Virtual blackbody (wedge) method | Use a wedge/cavity geometry on the sample or fixture to create an effective emissivity near unity, providing a local blackbody-like reference for calibration or validation. | Reduces emissivity uncertainty without coating; useful for industrial strip/coil practice; provides an internal reference. | Sensitive to alignment and geometry; may be difficult to implement in compact or moving induction setups; only samples local geometry. | (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000001) |
| Neural network / AI approaches | Use multispectral data-driven inversion to recognize emissivity behavior and estimate temperature/emissivity without prescribing a fixed analytical emissivity model. | Can capture nonlinear emissivity behavior and automate model selection; promising for online monitoring and process control. | Requires representative training data; limited interpretability; robustness outside the training domain is uncertain. | (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000009) |
| Thin-film optics / geometric optics modeling | Model emissivity changes from oxide-film thickness, refractive index, roughness, nodules, and scattering using thin-film interference, geometric optics, or hybrid ray-tracing approaches. | Physically grounded correction path for annealing steels where oxide growth dominates emissivity; explains wavelength-dependent oscillations and atmosphere effects. | Requires optical constants, oxide morphology/thickness, and often process-specific validation; difficult to use as a standalone real-time correction. | (pqac-00000039, pqac-00000040, pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042) |


*Table: This table summarizes the main calibration and correction approaches used to manage emissivity-driven uncertainty in pyrometry during induction heating and annealing of metals. It compares how each method works, where it helps most, and its practical limitations.*

function.arguments:
{"answer": "# Emissivity and Pyrometry Uncertainty During Induction Heating and Annealing of Metallic Samples: A Literature Review

## 1. Introduction

Non-contact temperature measurement via radiation pyrometry is essential for controlling induction heating and annealing of metallic samples, particularly steel and nickel-based superalloys. However, the accuracy of pyrometric temperature retrieval is fundamentally limited by uncertainty in the spectral emissivity of the target surface, which is itself a function of temperature, wavelength, surface condition, and processing atmosphere (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002). This review summarizes the principal sources of emissivity uncertainty, the resulting pyrometry errors, and the calibration and correction methods documented in the literature.

## 2. Sources of Emissivity Uncertainty

### 2.1 Intrinsic Material Properties

The spectral emissivity of metals is governed by their electrical conductivity through the Drude model and Hagen–Rubens relations, which predict both wavelength and temperature dependence. A crossover (X-point) wavelength exists where the sign of the temperature coefficient of emissivity changes (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000007). For nickel-based superalloys such as Inconel 625, Inconel 718, and Waspaloy, emissivity depends strongly on alloy composition, surface roughness, and the spectral range of the pyrometer. Experimental studies have confirmed that emissivity varies with temperature and that surface oxidation at elevated temperatures produces an oxide matte that modifies radiative properties (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016).

### 2.2 Surface Oxidation and Thin-Film Interference

During annealing, selective oxidation of alloying elements (Mn, Si, Cr, Al) produces oxide layers of varying composition, morphology, and thickness (typically 40–320 nm for advanced high-strength steels). When the oxide film thickness is comparable to or smaller than the pyrometry wavelength, thin-film interference (constructive and destructive interference at film interfaces) produces wavelength-dependent oscillations in spectral emissivity (pqac-00000039, pqac-00000040). The effect is strongest at shorter wavelengths (below ~2 μm) and for uniform, low-refractive-index films. At longer wavelengths where λ >> d, surface roughness and nodular oxide morphology dominate via random scattering, generally increasing emissivity (pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042). The annealing atmosphere (dew point) controls the extent of oxidation; higher dew points promote thicker oxides and higher emissivity, particularly below 2 μm (pqac-00000041, pqac-00000042). For austenitic stainless steels, short anneals produce thin chromium oxide layers while longer times yield iron oxide deposits over a protective sublayer, with emissivity increasing with both temperature and holding time (pqac-00000045, pqac-00000046).

### 2.3 Surface Roughness and Topography

Surface roughness relative to the measurement wavelength (σ/λ) enhances multiple scattering and increases effective emissivity. This effect compounds with oxide morphology changes during processing and makes emissivity spatially and temporally variable (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000007).

### 2.4 Environmental and Instrumental Factors

Reflected radiation from hot furnace surroundings, atmospheric absorption and emission along the optical path, incomplete filling of the pyrometer field of view, and viewing-angle dependence (significant above ~75°) all introduce additional measurement errors (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000004). At temperatures below ~300 °C, background and reflected radiation can dominate the signal (pqac-00000008).

## 3. Quantitative Uncertainty Estimates

Reported uncertainties depend strongly on the method and surface conditions. For steel processing, Bayesian pyrometry credibility intervals range from ±14 K to ±31 K depending on annealing dew point—far larger than the industrial targets of ±2–4 °C desired by steelmakers (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000026). Discrepancies between ex situ and in situ emissivity measurements can produce relative temperature errors up to ~10% (pqac-00000026). For general pyrometer instruments, reported measurement uncertainties are typically 1–3% (pqac-00000051), while emissivity measurement uncertainties for specific materials can reach 3–14% for steel and up to 18% expanded uncertainty for nickel sheet (pqac-00000004). For carbon steel Q235, emissivity uncertainty due to surface oxidation alone contributes 1.5–7.1% uncertainty in emissivity and 1.7–5.8 K uncertainty in temperature (Shi et al. 2015). At 2000 K, a surface assumed to have emissivity 0.5 must actually lie between 0.45 and 0.55 for 1% temperature accuracy (pqac-00000019). For 316L powder steel, emissivity varied from 0.33 to 0.58 over 59–900 °C, with maximum relative combined temperature measurement uncertainty not exceeding 5.5% (Cullinan et al. 2025).

## 4. Calibration and Correction Methods

The following table summarizes the principal calibration and correction methods identified in the literature:

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

### 4.1 Blackbody Reference Calibration

Pyrometers are calibrated against traceable blackbody sources prior to deployment, with instrument response factors grouped into a calibration constant. Periodic recalibration is standard industrial practice. The measurement model accounts for emitted, reflected, and extraneous radiation components, with reflected furnace radiation correctable if surroundings temperature is known (pqac-00000013).

### 4.2 Thermocouple Cross-Calibration

A direct and widely used approach involves adjusting the pyrometer emissivity setting until its reading matches a thermocouple reference temperature measured on the same sample. This method was employed for Inconel 625, Inconel 718, and Waspaloy using embedded thermocouples in blind holes (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000015). For annealing furnace applications, matching pyrometer and thermocouple readings provides in situ emissivity calibration (pqac-00000045).

### 4.3 Ratio (Dual-Wavelength) Pyrometry

Two-color pyrometry estimates temperature from the ratio of spectral irradiances at two wavelengths, canceling common factors such as atmospheric absorption and partial field-of-view filling (pqac-00000012). Methods such as TRACE build experimentally derived reciprocal emissivity functions to improve accuracy. However, the method requires a known, stable emissivity ratio between the two wavelengths, and oxide growth can violate this assumption (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000001).

### 4.4 Multi-Wavelength Pyrometry

Using three or more wavelengths, emissivity is modeled as a polynomial, exponential, or log-linear function of wavelength. Temperature and emissivity model coefficients are solved simultaneously using least-squares methods. The approach requires n ≥ m+2 wavelengths (where m is the polynomial order) to avoid overfitting (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000010). Multi-wavelength methods are robust for uncontaminated surfaces but can fail when emissivity behavior is complex or rapidly evolving due to oxidation (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000000).

### 4.5 Bayesian (Stochastic) Pyrometry

A Bayesian framework treats emissivity, calibration coefficients, and measurement noise as random variables with prior distributions. Temperature is inferred as a maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimate with credibility intervals that explicitly quantify uncertainty (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000028). Multivariate normal (MVN) priors for emissivity can be constructed from ex situ or in situ measurements. In situ-based priors yielded ±21 °C credibility intervals for peak annealing temperature estimates, while conditional emissivity priors that condition on dew point and equivalent blackbody temperature vectors have been proposed to further narrow posterior widths (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025). Stage-specific priors (different for heating vs. soaking) are recommended to balance robustness and precision (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000027).

### 4.6 Graphite Coating Method

Applying a thin graphite spray coating with known high emissivity (ε ≈ 0.97 ± 0.03 at 1064 nm below 2250 K) simplifies radiometric temperature retrieval in induction-heating setups by imposing a near-known emissivity boundary condition (pqac-00000032). This approach has been used at PTB for dynamic specific-heat and emissivity measurements up to ~2000 K, though coating degradation above ~2000 K limits the usable range (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000033).

### 4.7 Virtual Blackbody (Wedge) Method

In industrial continuous annealing and galvanizing lines, a wedge geometry creates a virtual blackbody with effective emissivity near unity, providing an internal calibration reference. While effective, this approach is sensitive to alignment and geometric precision (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000014).

### 4.8 Neural Network and AI Approaches

Back-propagation neural networks have been applied to multi-wavelength pyrometry data to automatically recognize linear and nonlinear emissivity models without assuming a specific functional form (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000009). Expert-system approaches using knowledge representation and decision logic have also been documented. For advanced high-strength steel coils, artificial neural networks have been used to predict spectral emissivity variations from pre-annealing surface properties, enabling on-line emissivity prediction and pyrometry updates (pqac-00000001).

### 4.9 Physics-Based Emissivity Modeling

Thin-film optics models (using complex refractive indices and film thickness), geometric optics approximation (GOA) ray-tracing, and hybrid approaches have been developed to predict emissivity changes arising from oxide scale growth during annealing. These models can explain wavelength-dependent emissivity oscillations and atmosphere-dependent emissivity evolution, providing physics-based correction factors for pyrometry (pqac-00000039, pqac-00000040, pqac-00000043, pqac-00000044). Parabolic oxidation kinetics with Arrhenius-derived parameters have been coupled to emissivity regression models to predict scale growth and its effect on emissivity during stainless steel annealing (pqac-00000045, pqac-00000046).

## 5. Induction Heating Specific Considerations

Induction heating introduces particular challenges and opportunities for pyrometry. The electromagnetic coupling can produce non-uniform temperature distributions, and the rapid heating rates require fast-response pyrometers. At PTB, a dedicated induction heating system (Ambrell EASYHEAT, max 6 kW, 240 kHz) was implemented for dynamic emissivity measurements with controlled drift below 150 mK/min at 1250 K, using v-grooved Nextel-painted chamber walls to reduce stray reflections (pqac-00000032). Graphite susceptor rings can assist heating of poorly inductive samples (pqac-00000032). The uncertainty evaluation employed adaptive Monte Carlo methods, and the dominant uncertainty contribution was from coating emissivity (~80% of the total specific heat uncertainty) (pqac-00000033).

## 6. References (BibTeX)

The following BibTeX entries compile the key references identified in this review:

<artifact id="artifact-01"/>

## 7. Conclusions

Emissivity uncertainty remains the dominant source of pyrometry error during induction heating and annealing of steel and nickel alloys. The primary physical mechanisms driving emissivity variability are surface oxidation (including thin-film interference), roughness evolution, and the intrinsic temperature-wavelength dependence of metallic emissivity. A range of calibration and correction methods are available, from traditional approaches (blackbody calibration, thermocouple cross-referencing) to advanced techniques (Bayesian inference, multi-wavelength pyrometry with emissivity compensation, neural networks). The most promising recent developments combine physics-based emissivity models with stochastic frameworks (Bayesian pyrometry with conditional priors) to provide both improved temperature estimates and explicit uncertainty quantification. However, achieving industrial accuracy targets (±2–4 °C) remains challenging, particularly when surface conditions evolve rapidly during processing."}

answer.answer:
# Emissivity and Pyrometry Uncertainty During Induction Heating and Annealing of Metallic Samples: A Literature Review

## 1. Introduction

Non-contact temperature measurement via radiation pyrometry is essential for controlling induction heating and annealing of metallic samples, particularly steel and nickel-based superalloys. However, the accuracy of pyrometric temperature retrieval is fundamentally limited by uncertainty in the spectral emissivity of the target surface, which is itself a function of temperature, wavelength, surface condition, and processing atmosphere (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 63-66, suleiman2023effectofdewa pages 9-10). This review summarizes the principal sources of emissivity uncertainty, the resulting pyrometry errors, and the calibration and correction methods documented in the literature.

## 2. Sources of Emissivity Uncertainty

### 2.1 Intrinsic Material Properties

The spectral emissivity of metals is governed by their electrical conductivity through the Drude model and Hagen–Rubens relations, which predict both wavelength and temperature dependence. A crossover (X-point) wavelength exists where the sign of the temperature coefficient of emissivity changes (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 63-66, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 63-66). For nickel-based superalloys such as Inconel 625, Inconel 718, and Waspaloy, emissivity depends strongly on alloy composition, surface roughness, and the spectral range of the pyrometer. Experimental studies have confirmed that emissivity varies with temperature and that surface oxidation at elevated temperatures produces an oxide matte that modifies radiative properties (kieruj2016determinationofemissivity pages 1-2, kieruj2016determinationofemissivity pages 2-4).

### 2.2 Surface Oxidation and Thin-Film Interference

During annealing, selective oxidation of alloying elements (Mn, Si, Cr, Al) produces oxide layers of varying composition, morphology, and thickness (typically 40–320 nm for advanced high-strength steels). When the oxide film thickness is comparable to or smaller than the pyrometry wavelength, thin-film interference (constructive and destructive interference at film interfaces) produces wavelength-dependent oscillations in spectral emissivity (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 63-66, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 63-66). The effect is strongest at shorter wavelengths (below ~2 μm) and for uniform, low-refractive-index films. At longer wavelengths where λ >> d, surface roughness and nodular oxide morphology dominate via random scattering, generally increasing emissivity (suleiman2023effectofdew pages 2-5, suleiman2023effectofdewa pages 2-5). The annealing atmosphere (dew point) controls the extent of oxidation; higher dew points promote thicker oxides and higher emissivity, particularly below 2 μm (suleiman2023effectofdew pages 2-5, suleiman2023effectofdewa pages 2-5). For austenitic stainless steels, short anneals produce thin chromium oxide layers while longer times yield iron oxide deposits over a protective sublayer, with emissivity increasing with both temperature and holding time (lassila2023effectofscale pages 64-69, lassila2023effectofscalea pages 64-69).

### 2.3 Surface Roughness and Topography

Surface roughness relative to the measurement wavelength (σ/λ) enhances multiple scattering and increases effective emissivity. This effect compounds with oxide morphology changes during processing and makes emissivity spatially and temporally variable (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 63-66, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 63-66).

### 2.4 Environmental and Instrumental Factors

Reflected radiation from hot furnace surroundings, atmospheric absorption and emission along the optical path, incomplete filling of the pyrometer field of view, and viewing-angle dependence (significant above ~75°) all introduce additional measurement errors (grujic2023areviewof pages 4-6, giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 10-11). At temperatures below ~300 °C, background and reflected radiation can dominate the signal (giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 11-12).

## 3. Quantitative Uncertainty Estimates

Reported uncertainties depend strongly on the method and surface conditions. For steel processing, Bayesian pyrometry credibility intervals range from ±14 K to ±31 K depending on annealing dew point—far larger than the industrial targets of ±2–4 °C desired by steelmakers (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 156-160, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 156-160). Discrepancies between ex situ and in situ emissivity measurements can produce relative temperature errors up to ~10% (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 156-160). For general pyrometer instruments, reported measurement uncertainties are typically 1–3% (giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 11-11), while emissivity measurement uncertainties for specific materials can reach 3–14% for steel and up to 18% expanded uncertainty for nickel sheet (giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 10-11). For carbon steel Q235, emissivity uncertainty due to surface oxidation alone contributes 1.5–7.1% uncertainty in emissivity and 1.7–5.8 K uncertainty in temperature (Shi et al. 2015). At 2000 K, a surface assumed to have emissivity 0.5 must actually lie between 0.45 and 0.55 for 1% temperature accuracy (cunnington1973developmentoftechniques pages 56-61). For 316L powder steel, emissivity varied from 0.33 to 0.58 over 59–900 °C, with maximum relative combined temperature measurement uncertainty not exceeding 5.5% (Cullinan et al. 2025).

## 4. Calibration and Correction Methods

The following table summarizes the principal calibration and correction methods identified in the literature:

| Method | Principle | Advantages | Limitations | Key References |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Blackbody reference calibration | Calibrate pyrometers/IR systems against a traceable blackbody or blackbody-equivalent source; instrument response factors are folded into calibration constants, and reflected/background radiation can be corrected if surroundings are characterized. | Traceable baseline; standard industrial practice; improves absolute accuracy; supports periodic recalibration and uncertainty control. | Does not solve process-time emissivity drift from oxidation/roughness; field conditions can differ from lab calibration; background reflections and alignment still matter. | (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 53-57, giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 11-12, giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 10-11) |
| Thermocouple cross-calibration | Adjust pyrometer emissivity or correction curves until non-contact readings match embedded or attached thermocouple temperatures under controlled conditions. | Simple and widely used; practical for alloy- and surface-specific emissivity tuning; useful for furnace and annealing trials. | Thermocouples perturb the specimen and may not represent true surface temperature during fast transients or induction heating; contact methods are difficult on moving/rotating parts. | (kieruj2016determinationofemissivity pages 2-4, kieruj2016determinationofemissivity pages 1-2, lassila2023effectofscale pages 64-69) |
| Ratio / dual-wavelength pyrometry | Infer temperature from the ratio of radiances at two wavelengths so common factors partly cancel; accuracy depends on a stable or known emissivity ratio between the two bands. | Less sensitive than single-color pyrometry to absolute emissivity, attenuation, and partial field-of-view filling; fast and practical for dynamic heating. | Not truly emissivity-independent if emissivity ratio changes with oxide growth or wavelength spacing; errors increase when surfaces are non-gray or rapidly evolving. | (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 60-63, narayanan2023aninvestigationinto pages 36-40, ren2019emissivitycalibrationmethod pages 6-8, giulietti2025spectralemissivitymeasurement pages 11-12) |
| Multi-wavelength pyrometry with polynomial emissivity models | Use 3+ wavelengths and fit temperature simultaneously with emissivity represented by linear/log-linear/polynomial/exponential wavelength functions, often by least squares. | Can compensate evolving wavelength dependence better than ratio pyrometry; can estimate both emissivity trend and temperature; suitable for complex metallic surfaces. | Sensitive to noise, model-form error, and overfitting; performance degrades when oxidation/roughness causes behavior outside the assumed emissivity model. | (narayanan2023aninvestigationinto pages 36-40, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 60-63, grujic2023areviewof pages 9-10) |
| Bayesian / stochastic pyrometry | Treat emissivity, calibration coefficients, and noise probabilistically; infer posterior temperature with MAP estimate and credibility intervals using ex situ, in situ, or conditional emissivity priors. | Explicit uncertainty quantification; handles poorly known emissivity; adaptable to dew point, alloy, and process state; can outperform deterministic methods when emissivity varies strongly. | Strongly dependent on prior quality; wide intervals remain when emissivity is weakly constrained; computationally heavier and may still miss industrial accuracy targets. | (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 156-160, suleiman2023effectofdew pages 7-9, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 160-163, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 111-115) |
| Graphite coating method | Apply a thin high-emissivity coating with known spectral emissivity so radiometric temperature retrieval is less sensitive to the substrate’s unknown emissivity. | Effective way to impose a near-known emissivity; useful in induction-heated high-temperature metrology; simplifies inversion and uncertainty analysis. | Coating can degrade or spall at high temperature; may alter thermal behavior; not always acceptable for process samples or production parts. | (urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 1-4, urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 4-8, urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 14-16) |
| Virtual blackbody (wedge) method | Use a wedge/cavity geometry on the sample or fixture to create an effective emissivity near unity, providing a local blackbody-like reference for calibration or validation. | Reduces emissivity uncertainty without coating; useful for industrial strip/coil practice; provides an internal reference. | Sensitive to alignment and geometry; may be difficult to implement in compact or moving induction setups; only samples local geometry. | (narayanan2023aninvestigationinto pages 36-40) |
| Neural network / AI approaches | Use multispectral data-driven inversion to recognize emissivity behavior and estimate temperature/emissivity without prescribing a fixed analytical emissivity model. | Can capture nonlinear emissivity behavior and automate model selection; promising for online monitoring and process control. | Requires representative training data; limited interpretability; robustness outside the training domain is uncertain. | (grujic2023areviewof pages 9-10, grujic2023areviewof pages 26-27) |
| Thin-film optics / geometric optics modeling | Model emissivity changes from oxide-film thickness, refractive index, roughness, nodules, and scattering using thin-film interference, geometric optics, or hybrid ray-tracing approaches. | Physically grounded correction path for annealing steels where oxide growth dominates emissivity; explains wavelength-dependent oscillations and atmosphere effects. | Requires optical constants, oxide morphology/thickness, and often process-specific validation; difficult to use as a standalone real-time correction. | (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 63-66, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 63-66, suleiman2023effectofdew pages 2-5, suleiman2023effectofdewa pages 2-5) |


*Table: This table summarizes the main calibration and correction approaches used to manage emissivity-driven uncertainty in pyrometry during induction heating and annealing of metals. It compares how each method works, where it helps most, and its practical limitations.*

### 4.1 Blackbody Reference Calibration

Pyrometers are calibrated against traceable blackbody sources prior to deployment, with instrument response factors grouped into a calibration constant. Periodic recalibration is standard industrial practice. The measurement model accounts for emitted, reflected, and extraneous radiation components, with reflected furnace radiation correctable if surroundings temperature is known (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 53-57).

### 4.2 Thermocouple Cross-Calibration

A direct and widely used approach involves adjusting the pyrometer emissivity setting until its reading matches a thermocouple reference temperature measured on the same sample. This method was employed for Inconel 625, Inconel 718, and Waspaloy using embedded thermocouples in blind holes (kieruj2016determinationofemissivity pages 2-4, kieruj2016determinationofemissivity pages 1-2). For annealing furnace applications, matching pyrometer and thermocouple readings provides in situ emissivity calibration (lassila2023effectofscale pages 64-69).

### 4.3 Ratio (Dual-Wavelength) Pyrometry

Two-color pyrometry estimates temperature from the ratio of spectral irradiances at two wavelengths, canceling common factors such as atmospheric absorption and partial field-of-view filling (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 60-63). Methods such as TRACE build experimentally derived reciprocal emissivity functions to improve accuracy. However, the method requires a known, stable emissivity ratio between the two wavelengths, and oxide growth can violate this assumption (narayanan2023aninvestigationinto pages 36-40).

### 4.4 Multi-Wavelength Pyrometry

Using three or more wavelengths, emissivity is modeled as a polynomial, exponential, or log-linear function of wavelength. Temperature and emissivity model coefficients are solved simultaneously using least-squares methods. The approach requires n ≥ m+2 wavelengths (where m is the polynomial order) to avoid overfitting (narayanan2023aninvestigationinto pages 36-40, grujic2023areviewof pages 9-10). Multi-wavelength methods are robust for uncontaminated surfaces but can fail when emissivity behavior is complex or rapidly evolving due to oxidation (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 60-63, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 63-66).

### 4.5 Bayesian (Stochastic) Pyrometry

A Bayesian framework treats emissivity, calibration coefficients, and measurement noise as random variables with prior distributions. Temperature is inferred as a maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimate with credibility intervals that explicitly quantify uncertainty (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 156-160, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 160-163, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 111-115). Multivariate normal (MVN) priors for emissivity can be constructed from ex situ or in situ measurements. In situ-based priors yielded ±21 °C credibility intervals for peak annealing temperature estimates, while conditional emissivity priors that condition on dew point and equivalent blackbody temperature vectors have been proposed to further narrow posterior widths (suleiman2023effectofdew pages 7-9, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 160-163). Stage-specific priors (different for heating vs. soaking) are recommended to balance robustness and precision (suleiman2023effectofdew pages 7-9, suleiman2023effectofdewa pages 7-9).

### 4.6 Graphite Coating Method

Applying a thin graphite spray coating with known high emissivity (ε ≈ 0.97 ± 0.03 at 1064 nm below 2250 K) simplifies radiometric temperature retrieval in induction-heating setups by imposing a near-known emissivity boundary condition (urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 4-8). This approach has been used at PTB for dynamic specific-heat and emissivity measurements up to ~2000 K, though coating degradation above ~2000 K limits the usable range (urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 1-4, urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 14-16).

### 4.7 Virtual Blackbody (Wedge) Method

In industrial continuous annealing and galvanizing lines, a wedge geometry creates a virtual blackbody with effective emissivity near unity, providing an internal calibration reference. While effective, this approach is sensitive to alignment and geometric precision (narayanan2023aninvestigationinto pages 36-40).

### 4.8 Neural Network and AI Approaches

Back-propagation neural networks have been applied to multi-wavelength pyrometry data to automatically recognize linear and nonlinear emissivity models without assuming a specific functional form (grujic2023areviewof pages 9-10, grujic2023areviewof pages 26-27). Expert-system approaches using knowledge representation and decision logic have also been documented. For advanced high-strength steel coils, artificial neural networks have been used to predict spectral emissivity variations from pre-annealing surface properties, enabling on-line emissivity prediction and pyrometry updates (narayanan2023aninvestigationinto pages 36-40).

### 4.9 Physics-Based Emissivity Modeling

Thin-film optics models (using complex refractive indices and film thickness), geometric optics approximation (GOA) ray-tracing, and hybrid approaches have been developed to predict emissivity changes arising from oxide scale growth during annealing. These models can explain wavelength-dependent emissivity oscillations and atmosphere-dependent emissivity evolution, providing physics-based correction factors for pyrometry (suleiman2025improvingpyrometryof pages 63-66, suleiman2025improvingpyrometryofa pages 63-66, suleiman2023effectofdewa pages 9-10, suleiman2023effectofdew pages 9-10). Parabolic oxidation kinetics with Arrhenius-derived parameters have been coupled to emissivity regression models to predict scale growth and its effect on emissivity during stainless steel annealing (lassila2023effectofscale pages 64-69, lassila2023effectofscalea pages 64-69).

## 5. Induction Heating Specific Considerations

Induction heating introduces particular challenges and opportunities for pyrometry. The electromagnetic coupling can produce non-uniform temperature distributions, and the rapid heating rates require fast-response pyrometers. At PTB, a dedicated induction heating system (Ambrell EASYHEAT, max 6 kW, 240 kHz) was implemented for dynamic emissivity measurements with controlled drift below 150 mK/min at 1250 K, using v-grooved Nextel-painted chamber walls to reduce stray reflections (urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 4-8). Graphite susceptor rings can assist heating of poorly inductive samples (urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 4-8). The uncertainty evaluation employed adaptive Monte Carlo methods, and the dominant uncertainty contribution was from coating emissivity (~80% of the total specific heat uncertainty) (urban2022dynamicmeasurementof pages 14-16).
